content.text: Network isolation restricts the inbound and outbound network access available to an agent's execution environment. By controlling exactly which external endpoints an agent can reach, operators prevent data exfiltration, command-and-control communication, and the lateral movement that makes a compromised sandbox dangerous.
What it is

Network isolation for AI agents means the execution environment has no unrestricted access to the internet or internal networks. Access is controlled through a combination of kernel-level policies (network namespaces, iptables/nftables), infrastructure-level controls (Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, security groups), and application-level proxies that enforce domain allowlists.
The strongest form is complete network removal (
--network none
in Docker), where the agent has no network interface at all. A middle ground is egress-only access through an authenticated proxy that enforces an explicit allowlist of permitted domains.
Why it matters

Network access is the primary exfiltration channel for a compromised agent. An agent whose sandbox is breached can exfiltrate data, receive attacker commands, or pivot to internal services — all via network connections. Without network isolation, execution sandboxing provides much weaker guarantees.

Network isolation is also the primary defense against prompt-injection attacks that try to make the model fetch resources from attacker-controlled URLs. If the agent's network cannot reach arbitrary hosts, the fetch fails regardless of what the model decides.
Benefits
Exfiltration prevention
— even if agent code reads sensitive data, it cannot transmit it outside the defined perimeter.
C2 channel blocking
— malicious code cannot phone home to attacker infrastructure to receive further instructions.
Lateral movement prevention
— the agent cannot probe or attack other internal services even if the sandbox itself is compromised.
Predictable blast radius
— the agent's impact is bounded to what it can do with the data it already has, not what it can fetch.
Compliance
— data residency requirements often mandate that certain data never leaves a specific network boundary.
Trade-offs
Reduced capability
— many legitimate agent tasks require web access (search, API calls, fetching documentation). Full isolation blocks these.
Allowlist maintenance burden
— as the agent's toolset grows, the allowlist must be kept in sync, or legitimate calls will fail unexpectedly.
DNS-based allowlists can be bypassed
— an attacker who controls a subdomain of an allowlisted domain can potentially serve malicious content.
Proxy latency
— routing all traffic through an egress proxy adds 1–10ms per request and introduces a single point of failure.
How it works
Layer 1: Network namespace (no network)

The strongest option: give the container no network namespace. The container has no loopback or external interface. Any syscall that attempts to create a socket fails immediately. This is appropriate for pure computation tasks — data analysis, code generation without external fetches.
Layer 2: Egress proxy with domain allowlist

When the agent needs controlled internet access, route all outbound traffic through an authenticating forward proxy (Squid, mitmproxy, or a cloud NAT gateway with URL filtering). The proxy enforces a domain allowlist — requests to unlisted domains are denied and logged. iptables rules on the sandbox host block direct connections that bypass the proxy.
Layer 3: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy

In Kubernetes, NetworkPolicy resources define which pods can talk to which other pods and which external CIDRs. Applying a default-deny-all policy to the agent's namespace, then explicitly allowing only the egress proxy's IP and port, gives infrastructure-level enforcement that is independent of the container runtime.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Agent network isolation showing sandbox, egress proxy with allowlist, external services, and audit log

content.text: All agent network traffic flows through an egress proxy that enforces domain allowlists. Direct connections are blocked at the iptables/NetworkPolicy layer. Every allow and deny is logged to an audit system.
When to use it
All code-execution agents, regardless of task — even "safe" code can make outbound connections.
Agents that process PII or confidential data — network isolation is a mandatory data residency control.
Multi-tenant platforms — isolation prevents one tenant's agent from reaching another tenant's internal endpoints.
Environments with high-value internal services (databases, secret stores) — blocking lateral movement to these is critical.
How to implement
Complete network removal (Docker)

# No network interface at all — strongest isolation
docker run \
  --network none \
  --rm \
  --read-only \
  --memory 256m \
  --cpus 0.5 \
  python:3.12-slim \
  python /code/script.py

# Verify: inside the container, the following all fail:
# socket.connect(...)  -> [Errno 101] Network is unreachable
# requests.get(...)    -> ConnectionError
# subprocess.run(['curl', ...]) -> curl: (6) Could not resolve host
Egress proxy with domain allowlist

# Squid proxy configuration (squid.conf)
# Only allow specific domains; deny everything else
"""
acl agent_sandbox src 10.0.100.0/24   # agent container subnet
acl allowed_domains dstdomain .openai.com .anthropic.com .s3.amazonaws.com
http_access allow agent_sandbox allowed_domains
http_access deny all
request_header_access Authorization allow all
access_log /var/log/squid/access.log combined
"""

# iptables rules on the host to force all agent traffic through proxy
# Block direct external connections (bypass attempts)
import subprocess

def configure_network_isolation(agent_subnet: str, proxy_ip: str, proxy_port: int = 3128):
    """Set iptables rules to force agent traffic through proxy."""
    rules = [
        # Allow established connections
        f"-A FORWARD -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT",
        # Allow agent -> proxy
        f"-A FORWARD -s {agent_subnet} -d {proxy_ip} -p tcp --dport {proxy_port} -j ACCEPT",
        # Block all other outbound from agent subnet
        f"-A FORWARD -s {agent_subnet} -j DROP",
    ]
    for rule in rules:
        subprocess.run(["iptables"] + rule.split(), check=True)
Kubernetes NetworkPolicy

# Default deny-all for agent namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: agent-default-deny
  namespace: agent-sandbox
spec:
  podSelector: {}   # applies to all pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow only egress to the proxy pod
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: agent-allow-proxy-egress
  namespace: agent-sandbox
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: agent
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              role: egress-proxy
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 3128
    # Also allow DNS (required for proxy to resolve domain names)
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
Real-world example

A security company built an AI threat-intelligence agent that crawled public vulnerability databases and correlated them with internal asset inventories. The agent ran in Kubernetes with a NetworkPolicy that allowed egress only to three specific external domains (NVD API, CISA KEV feed, CVE.org) via an egress proxy, plus the internal API for asset inventory.

When the agent encountered a prompt-injection attack embedded in a malicious advisory page that instructed it to "fetch http://attacker.com/c2.sh", the request was blocked at the proxy — the domain was not on the allowlist. The block was logged and triggered a security alert. The agent continued with a benign error message returned as the tool result.
Common mistakes
Allowlisting by IP instead of domain
— cloud service IPs rotate frequently. Allowlist domains and resolve them at the proxy, not at the firewall.
Forgetting DNS-over-HTTPS bypass
— if the sandbox can reach port 443, it may use DoH to resolve arbitrary hostnames, bypassing DNS-based controls.
Wildcard allowlisting
— allowing *.amazonaws.com gives access to any AWS-hosted attacker infrastructure. Be specific.
No egress audit logging
— allowed traffic can still be anomalous. Logging every request enables post-incident analysis.
Missing proxy authentication
— an unauthenticated proxy in the network can be abused by any process that can reach it, not just the sandboxed agent.
Best practices
Default deny everything, then explicitly allow only what the agent actually needs.
Use --network none for pure-compute tasks with no legitimate network needs.
Route all allowed traffic through an auditing egress proxy, not directly to the internet.
Log all allowed and denied requests to a SIEM. Alert on deny-burst patterns that indicate escape attempts.
Control DNS resolution: point the sandbox's DNS resolver to a filtering resolver that only resolves allowlisted domains.
Block UDP egress entirely unless specifically required — most DNS and NTP can be handled by the proxy.
Performance & scalability

A well-tuned egress proxy adds 1–5ms of latency per request, negligible for most agent workloads. The proxy itself can become a bottleneck when many agents make simultaneous external calls. Run the proxy as a replicated service behind a load balancer with connection pooling enabled.
Cache DNS resolutions at the proxy to reduce repeated lookups.
Apply per-agent rate limits at the proxy to prevent one agent from saturating the bandwidth available to others.
Use connection keepalives so repeated API calls reuse TLS sessions rather than renegotiating each time.
Tooling & ecosystem
Squid
— mature HTTP/HTTPS forward proxy with robust ACL and domain filtering. Easy to configure for allowlist enforcement.
mitmproxy
— programmable HTTPS proxy with Python scripting; enables custom filtering logic and full request/response logging.
Kubernetes NetworkPolicy + Cilium
— eBPF-based network policy enforcement with L7 filtering and built-in observability.
AWS VPC + NAT Gateway with URL filtering
— managed egress control in AWS; AWS Network Firewall provides domain-based filtering.
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Isolation
Flexibility
Auditability
--network none
Total
None
N/A
iptables allowlist
High
Low
Limited
Egress proxy + domain allowlist
High

Via Cilium
Security & reliability

Network isolation is only effective if it is enforced at a level the sandboxed code cannot influence. Kernel-level enforcement (namespaces, iptables, cgroups) cannot be bypassed from user space. Application-level controls like
REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE
or HTTP proxy environment variables can be overridden by code running inside the sandbox — do not rely on them alone.
Enforce isolation at the kernel (iptables) or infrastructure (NetworkPolicy) level — not at the application level.
Treat proxy unavailability as a hard block: the sandbox should fail closed (no network) rather than fall back to unrestricted access.
Rotate proxy credentials regularly to limit the damage from a stolen credential.
Mental model

Think of network isolation as a one-way airlock. The agent sits in the inner chamber. To reach the outside world, it must pass through a controlled passage (the egress proxy) that checks credentials, verifies the destination is allowed, and logs the transit. There is no direct door from the inner chamber to the outside — and no door at all from the outside in.
The key insight is that the agent should not know it is isolated. It sets
HTTP_PROXY

as instructed; its HTTP client routes through the proxy transparently. From the agent's perspective, it made a normal HTTP call. From the security perspective, that call was filtered, audited, and potentially denied — all without the agent being able to influence the enforcement logic.
Advanced patterns
Per-task dynamic allowlists

Static allowlists are simple but inflexible. A more advanced pattern generates a per-task allowlist from the task's declared tool requirements. Before the agent starts, the orchestrator inspects the tool schema, extracts the external domains each tool calls, and configures the proxy with exactly those domains for the lifetime of the task. When the task completes, the allowlist is revoked.

import contextlib

TOOL_DOMAIN_MAP = {
    "search_web": ["api.tavily.com", "api.serper.dev"],
    "fetch_paper": ["arxiv.org", "www.semanticscholar.org"],
    "call_llm": ["api.anthropic.com"],
}

@contextlib.contextmanager
def task_network_scope(tool_names: list[str], proxy_client):
    """
    Context manager that activates a minimal domain allowlist for the
    duration of a task and revokes it on exit.
    """
    domains: set[str] = set()
    for tool in tool_names:
        domains.update(TOOL_DOMAIN_MAP.get(tool, []))

    task_id = proxy_client.create_allowlist(list(domains))
    try:
        yield task_id
    finally:
        proxy_client.revoke_allowlist(task_id)

# Usage
with task_network_scope(["search_web", "call_llm"], proxy) as task_id:
    result = run_agent(task_id=task_id, user_request="...")
DNS sinkholing

For maximum control, route the sandbox's DNS resolver to a sinkhole DNS server that only resolves allowlisted domains and returns NXDOMAIN for everything else. This prevents resolution-based bypass attacks independently of the proxy. Combine with blocking UDP port 53 to external resolvers so the sandbox cannot use a public DNS service to bypass the sinkhole.
Testing network isolation

Configuration drift and infrastructure changes can silently weaken isolation. The only way to trust that your sandbox is actually isolated is to test it — both at deployment time and as part of ongoing CI checks. The goal is to verify that connections that
should
be blocked are blocked, and that connections that should be allowed still succeed.
What to verify
Blocked egress to arbitrary internet hosts
— a TCP connection attempt to an external IP on port 80/443 must fail.
Blocked DNS to external resolvers
— a UDP/TCP query to 8.8.8.8:53 must be dropped, not just redirected.
Allowed egress to the proxy
— an HTTP CONNECT to the proxy IP succeeds, confirming the proxy path is intact.
Allowed domains pass through the proxy
— an HTTPS request to an allowlisted domain returns a 2xx response.
Non-allowlisted domains are denied
— a request to a domain not on the allowlist returns a proxy 403/407 or a connection error.
Isolation test harness
Run these checks from inside the sandbox container to confirm that enforcement works from the perspective of code the agent would actually execute.

"""network_isolation_tests.py

Run this script INSIDE the sandboxed container to validate that network
isolation is actually enforced. Designed to be part of a CI smoke-test
run after every infrastructure change.
"""
import socket
import urllib.request
import urllib.error
import os
import sys

PROXY_HOST = os.environ.get("HTTPS_PROXY", "http://proxy:3128")
ALLOWED_DOMAIN = "api.anthropic.com"
BLOCKED_DOMAIN = "example.com"          # not on the allowlist
EXTERNAL_DNS = "8.8.8.8"


def assert_blocked(description: str, fn):
    """Assert that fn() raises a network error (connection refused/timeout)."""
    try:
        fn()
        print(f"  FAIL  {description} — connection succeeded (should be blocked)")
        return False
    except (OSError, urllib.error.URLError):
        print(f"  PASS  {description}")
        return True


def assert_allowed(description: str, fn):
    """Assert that fn() succeeds without a network error."""
    try:
        fn()
        print(f"  PASS  {description}")
        return True
    except (OSError, urllib.error.URLError) as e:
        print(f"  FAIL  {description} — {e}")
        return False


def test_direct_egress_blocked():
    """Direct TCP to 1.1.1.1:443 must be unreachable."""
    def connect():
        with socket.create_connection(("1.1.1.1", 443), timeout=3):
            pass
    return assert_blocked("Direct TCP egress to 1.1.1.1:443", connect)


def test_external_dns_blocked():
    """DNS query to external resolver 8.8.8.8 must be dropped."""
    def query():
        # Raw UDP DNS query for 'example.com' (minimal wire format)
        payload = (
            b"\xaa\xbb"  # transaction ID
            b"\x01\x00"  # standard query
            b"\x00\x01\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00"  # QDCOUNT=1
            b"\x07example\x03com\x00"
            b"\x00\x01\x00\x01"
        )
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
        sock.settimeout(3)
        sock.sendto(payload, (EXTERNAL_DNS, 53))
        sock.recv(512)  # should timeout / fail
    return assert_blocked("UDP DNS to external resolver 8.8.8.8:53", query)


def test_blocked_domain_denied():
    """Proxy must deny requests to domains not on the allowlist."""
    def fetch():
        proxy_handler = urllib.request.ProxyHandler({"https": PROXY_HOST})
        opener = urllib.request.build_opener(proxy_handler)
        opener.open(f"https://{BLOCKED_DOMAIN}/", timeout=5)
    return assert_blocked(f"Proxy denies {BLOCKED_DOMAIN}", fetch)


def test_allowed_domain_reachable():
    """Proxy must forward requests to allowlisted domains."""
    def fetch():
        proxy_handler = urllib.request.ProxyHandler({"https": PROXY_HOST})
        opener = urllib.request.build_opener(proxy_handler)
        # HEAD request to avoid downloading a response body
        req = urllib.request.Request(
            f"https://{ALLOWED_DOMAIN}/",
            method="HEAD"
        )
        opener.open(req, timeout=10)
    return assert_allowed(f"Proxy allows {ALLOWED_DOMAIN}", fetch)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("Network isolation smoke tests")
    print("-" * 40)
    results = [
        test_direct_egress_blocked(),
        test_external_dns_blocked(),
        test_blocked_domain_denied(),
        test_allowed_domain_reachable(),
    ]
    passed = sum(results)
    total = len(results)
    print("-" * 40)
    print(f"{passed}/{total} tests passed")
    sys.exit(0 if passed == total else 1)
Integrating into CI

Package the test harness as a Docker image that your pipeline spins up in the same network configuration as your production sandbox. Run it as a post-deployment gate — if any "should be blocked" test passes (meaning traffic leaked through), the deployment fails and the old configuration is preserved.

# .github/workflows/network-isolation-check.yml
jobs:
  isolation-smoke-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Start egress proxy
        run: docker compose up -d proxy

      - name: Run sandbox with isolation tests
        run: |
          docker run \
            --network agent-sandbox \
            --env HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy:3128 \
            --rm \
            my-sandbox-image \
            python network_isolation_tests.py
        # Non-zero exit code (from sys.exit(1)) fails the workflow

Running these checks in CI catches two common failure modes: a misconfigured iptables rule that opens egress after a kernel upgrade, and a NetworkPolicy rollback that happens when Kubernetes nodes are replaced during a cluster upgrade.
Key takeaways
Network isolation is the primary defense against data exfiltration and C2 communication from a compromised agent.
Use --network none for pure-compute tasks; use an egress proxy with a domain allowlist for agents that need controlled internet access.
Enforce isolation at the kernel or infrastructure level — application-level proxy settings are bypassable from inside the sandbox.
Log all allowed and denied traffic; alert on deny bursts that indicate escape attempts.
Avoid wildcard domain allowlists; be explicit about which endpoints the agent actually needs to reach.
Proxy unavailability must fail closed — never degrade to unrestricted access.
